coredata.dc:description: The complex interaction of lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose in the hydrothermal degradation progress of lignocellulose, has led to uncertainty in the hydrothermal synthesis of lignocellulose-based CQDs (LC-CQDs). This makes it difficult to identify the specific formation mechanism of LC-CQDs. To simplify the reaction system and comprehensively describe the formation of LC-CQDs, both lignin and hemicellulose, the main hydrothermal degradation products of lignocellulose, were used as precursor to simulate and explore the synthesis of LC-CQDs at different time intervals (2–12 h). First, different lignin models were employed for preparing CQDs to determine the key lignin structure that govern CQDs formation. G-type lignin-model based CQDs were shown to have higher fluorescence intensity than H- and S-type. Then, G-type lignin model and hemicellulose model (xylan) were used simultaneously hydrothermal to prepare LC-CQDs. The analysis shows that the carbon nucleus preferentially formed by the lignin provides growth sites for small molecules degraded from hemicellulose, which gradually grow around the carbon core over time, thus forming a “sunflower” structure of CQDs. The presence of a lignin model could effectively guide the small molecules toward CQDs formation instead of carbonization. Additionally, the CQDs exhibit good in-vivo imaging performance.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The complex interaction of lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose in the hydrothermal degradation progress of lignocellulose, has led to uncertainty in the hydrothermal synthesis of lignocellulose-based CQDs (LC-CQDs). This makes it difficult to identify the specific formation mechanism of LC-CQDs. To simplify the reaction system and comprehensively describe the formation of LC-CQDs, both lignin and hemicellulose, the main hydrothermal degradation products of lignocellulose, were used as precursor to simulate and explore the synthesis of LC-CQDs at different time intervals (2–12 h). First, different lignin models were employed for preparing CQDs to determine the key lignin structure that govern CQDs formation. G-type lignin-model based CQDs were shown to have higher fluorescence intensity than H- and S-type. Then, G-type lignin model and hemicellulose model (xylan) were used simultaneously hydrothermal to prepare LC-CQDs. The analysis shows that the carbon nucleus preferentially formed by the lignin provides growth sites for small molecules degraded from hemicellulose, which gradually grow around the carbon core over time, thus forming a “sunflower” structure of CQDs. The presence of a lignin model could effectively guide the small molecules toward CQDs formation instead of carbonization. Additionally, the CQDs exhibit good in-vivo imaging performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Research on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been ongoing for 18 years [1]. As a quasi-zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon nanomaterial with excellent biocompatibility and stable properties [2], CQDs have formed a relatively promising application system in biological imaging [3], fluorescent probes [4], and water pollutant sensing [5]. Researchers are also gradually expanding their applications to photocatalysis [6], drug delivery [7], and electrochemistry [8]. Hydrothermal carbonization (HTC) is a chemical synthesis method for processing raw materials at 120–280 °C in a high-pressure closed vessel [9]. Compared with the microwave method [10], chemical oxidation method [11], and pyrolysis deposition method [12], the HTC method offers the advantages of simple operation, low cost, environment friendly, and non-toxic [13], and is widely used in the preparation of CQDs [14]. Elemental carbon (graphite, carbon fibre, or carbon black) and organic compounds (citric acid) are the primary raw materials for the traditional preparation of CQDs [15–17]. In recent years, as the concept of environmental protection has taken root in society, researchers have tried to use more widely distributed, and renewable biomass resources for the preparation of CQDs and have successfully achieved a low-cost synthesis of CQDs. Lignocellulosic materials, which account for the largest proportion of biomass [18], have been proven to provide high-quality raw materials for the preparation of CQDs [19].

Lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose are three key components of lignocellulosic materials which are all carbon precursors for the preparation of CQDS [20,21]. Lignin and hemicellulose are usually binders in lignocellulosic materials that wrap around the cellulose skeleton. Due to temperature, pressure, and structure, the three component degradation paths and dissolution rates are quite different during HTC. Therefore, the interaction of these three components leads to uncertainty in lignocellulosic CQD synthesis using an HTC reaction system. Moreover, some researchers have found that during the HTC process, hemicellulose and lignin are the main degraded components, whereas degradation of cellulose is difficult. For example, Khalilia et al. [22] reported that mainly lignin (46.2 %–65.7 %), xylan (16.3 %–52.1 %), and a small amount of glucan (0.34 %–8.6 %) were degraded during the hydrothermal treatment of sorghum culms at 150–180 °C. Xu et al. [23] used 1 vol% [C2NH2MIm][NO3] ionic liquid to treat wheat straw at 180 °C and found that hemicellulose, lignin, and cellulose dissolution rates were 90.5 %, 28.5 %, and 4.5 %, respectively. Furthermore, some researchers [24] believe that the aldol condensation reaction between furfural or 5-hydroxymethyl furfural (HMF) derived from hemicellulose degradation and lignin-phenol material significantly affects the formation of the final product. Thus, in the commonly used short duration (<24 h) HTC of lignocellulose-based CQDs, the interaction between hemicellulose and lignin can be considered the determining factor for the formation and properties of CQDs. This preliminary concept has been presented in our previous review paper [25] but still needs to be further confirmed.

Lignin units of lignocellulose mainly include guaiacyl (G-type), syringyl (S-type), and p-hydroxyphenyl (H-type). In the HTC process, the ether bonds of lignin macromolecules are attacked by H+, fragmented, and then degraded into lignin trimers, dimers, or monomers [26]. Subsequently, as the pressure increases, these small lignin molecules re-polymerize and eventually form large-molecule CQDs [13]. Therefore, prior to exploring the interaction between lignin and hemicellulose, it is necessary to determine the key lignin units that dominate the final fluorescence of CQDs. Simultaneously, xylan, the main component of hemicellulose in hardwoods and grasses, is attacked by H+ in the HTC process and is easily cleaved into single sugar groups, which are then dehydrated to form furan compounds (furfural or 5-hydroxymethylfurfural) [24]. These compounds are further decomposed into organic acids and other small molecules, which are eventually condensed and polymerized to form different solid and liquid products. Because, the thermal stability of the glycan degradation products is poor, and aromatization reactions are challenging to occur in a simple HTC process. Therefore, the final products of xylan degradation are mainly carbon nanodots (CNDs) with a low degree of graphitization [27]. In summary, the hydrothermal degradation paths of lignin and hemicellulose and the pathways for the formation of CQDs are quite different. In the same hydrothermal process, lignin and hemicellulose could form their own corresponding CQDs, or they could act synergistly to form CQDs together? What role do these two components play in the formation of CQDs remains uncertain? What effect does the high aromaticity of lignin and disorder of hemicellulose have on the graphitization of CQDs are worthy of additional investigation.

In this work, G-, S-, and H-type lignin unit model-based CQDs were prepared using a one-pot HTC method. The physical, chemical and optical properties of these CQDs were characterized to determine the critical lignin units that dominated the fluorescence performance of lignin-based CQDs. Then, the formation of hemicellulose-based (xylan-) CQDs (X-CQDs) during the hydrothermal process was investigated. On this basis, the synergistic or antagonistic effects of lignin and xylan on the formation of CQDs were investigated by exploring their physicochemical properties and fluorescence performance at different HTC time intervals. Finally, the in-vivo imaging performance of CQDs was investigated. This study provides guidance for exploring the complex formation mechanism of hydrothermally prepared natural-lignocellulose-based CQDs.

Three lignin monomer models (G-type, ferulic acid; S-type, erucic acid; H-type, coumaric acid, 99 %), xylan (85 %), and quinine sulfate (98 %) were purchased from Alding Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), MEM medium, and trypsin were purchased from Biological Industries Co., Ltd. (Israel). The reagents were used as received. Ultrapure water was prepared using Milli-Q® ultrapure water purification system (Merck KGaA, Germany).

The lignin model (0.1 g) and 30 mL of ultrapure water were added to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) reactor. After uniform mixing, the reactor was heated in an oven from room temperature to 180 °C and maintained for 12 h. Subsequently, the reactor was immediately cooled to room temperature in an ice water bath. Next, the reactant was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane to remove insoluble matter. The resulting liquid products were dialyzed in deionized water for five days using a dialysis bag with an intercepted molecular weight of 500 Da, and the dialysate was changed every 6 h. Finally, the dialyzed samples were concentrated using a parallel evaporator and freeze-dried into a powder, which was the lignin-model-based CQD. The CQDs prepared using the G-, S-, and H-type lignin models were denoted as G-CQDs-12, S-CQDs-12, and H-CQDs-12, respectively.

The preparation and purification conditions of the hemicellulosic (xylan)-based CQDs were the same as those described in Section “Preparation of lignin model-based CQDs,” but the precursor, in this case, was 0.1 g xylan, and the reaction time was changed to explore the formation process of xylan-based CQDs. Different xylan-based CQDs were prepared with HTC times of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 h and denoted as X-CQDs-X, i.e., X-CQDs-2, X-CQDs-4, X-CQDs-6, X-CQDs-8, X-CQDs-10, and X-CQDs-12, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lignocellulose-based CQDs using key lignin unit model and xylan as a precursor
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 g of the lignin model (G-Type, which was proved to be the key lignin unit model in Section “Preparation of lignin model-based CQDs”) and ultrapure water (30 mL) were added to a PTFE reactor. The reaction and purification conditions were the same as those described in Section “Preparation of hemicellulosic (xylan)-based CQDs.” The obtained CQDs with HTC times of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12 h were denoted as GX-CQDs-X, i.e., GX-CQDs-2, GX-CQDs-4, GX-CQDs-6, GX-CQDs-8, GX-CQDs-10, and GX-CQDs-12, respectively. A schematic for the preparation of CQDs is shown in Scheme 1 .

The quantum yields (QYs) of the CQDs were calculated using quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.05 mol/L sulfuric acid; QY = 54 %) as a reference. The QY of the CQDs was calculated at the optimal excitation wavelength for each sample using the following formula:
(1)
QY =
QY qs
·

S qs

A
·
ƞ 2

,
where QY is the quantum yield of the CQDs, qs is the quinine sulfate reference solution, S is the integral area of the fluorescence peak in the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the CQDs, A is the absorbance of the CQDs at the excitation wavelength, and ƞ represents the refractive index of the solution.

A Fourier transform infrared (FTIR; Bruker TENSOR II, Germany) spectrometer operating in the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) mode was used to elucidate the chemical structure of the CQDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific™ K-alpha+, USA) was used to measure the carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen contents and functional group compositions on the surfaces of the CQDs. The full-scan XPS spectra were recorded first, followed by the narrow-scan for C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s spectra. The pristine- and modified-X-CQD microstructures and the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI TECNAI G2 F30, USA) operating at 300 kV acceleration. The fluoroluminescence (FL) of the CQDs was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC, Hitachi, Japan). During the FL measurements, the slit widths of the excitation and emission lights were 5 nm and 10 nm, and ordinal-valued PL spectra were used to quantify the fluorescence intensity. An ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Hitachi u-4100 UV–vis, Japan) was used to obtain the UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs. A Raman spectrometer (LabRam HR Evolution, France) was used to determine the degree of graphitization of the CQDs.

The cytotoxicity of GX-CQDs-12 sample was first tested using CKK-8 method. The GX-CQDs-12 were dissolved in minimal essential medium (MEM) containing 10 % FBS with the concentration of 0.1, 0.5 and 1.0 mg/mL, respectively. The Hela cells in logarithmic growth phase were taken, counted and then inoculated into the above prepared mediums. The mediums were incubated at 37 °C under the condition of 5 % CO2 for 12 h, and the cell viability before and after the experiment was calculated to reflect cytotoxicity. Nude mice were adaptively fed for one week. In the logarithmic growth phase, HeLa cells were digested with trypsin to obtain single-cell suspensions. After counting the cells, the cell concentration was adjusted to 2 × 107 cells/mL. The cells (100 μL) were injected into the tumor implantation site on the right flank of the outer thigh of the nude mice. A nude mouse model was established when the tumor grew to 100–150 mm2. GX-CQDs-12 was dissolved in physiological saline to prepare a solution with 500 μg/mL concentration and then injected into the tail vein of nude mice with an injection volume of 100 μL. The NightOwl LB 983 three-dimensional small animal imaging system used in-vivo fluorescence imaging. The observation time was 10 min before and after injection and 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, and 24 h after injection. The mice were euthanized, the main organs (heart, liver, lung, kidney, tumor, and spleen) were removed, and their fluorescence images were obtained using the same three-dimensional small animal imaging system. Simultaneously, another nude mouse without dissection was subjected to the same experimental procedure to observe the toxicity of GX-CQDs-12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of key lignin unit type

ce:section.ce:para: The microscopic morphologies and particle size distributions of the three lignin-based CQDs were studied using TEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 1 . Obvious CQD structures can be observed in the HTC products of the G-type and H-type lignin models. As shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (c), at lower resolutions, the G-CQDs-12 and H-CQDs-12 appear to be evenly dispersed in the field of view with spherical and granular structures. At higher resolutions, some lattice fringes can be observed on the surfaces of G-CQDs-12 and H-CQDs-12, and the lattice spacing in both the samples is 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) in-plane graphene lattice [28]. These results indicate that G-CQDs-12 and H-CQDs-12 have a complete graphite structure, and H-CQDs-12 has a denser structure than G-CQDs-12. One hundred independent particles were randomly selected, and the particle size distribution diagrams of G-CQDs-12 and H-CQDs-12 were determined using Nano Measurer 1.2 software, as shown in the Fig. 1 (b) and (d). The diameter distribution of G-CQDs-12 and H-CQDs-12 is between 1.2–3.2 nm and 0.6–2.8 nm, respectively, and their corresponding average particle size is 2.00 nm and 1.34 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 (e), there are no apparent CQD structures (temporarily still called S-CQDs-12) in the HTC products of the S-type lignin model, which might be due to the symmetric dimethoxy side chain in the S-type lignin unit hindering the polymerization of the lignin benzene ring. The HTC products might be an irregular conjugated polymer resulting from the esterification or etherification reaction between the hydroxyl groups of the S-type lignin model.

The optical properties of CQDs are important evaluation standards for fluorescent materials. Fig. 2 (a) shows the UV–vis spectra of the G-CQDs, S-CQDs, and H-CQDs. The weak absorption peak near 230 nm is attributed to the n − n* transition of aromatic CC, and the strong absorption peak near 280 nm is attributed to the π − π* transition of CC and the n − π* transition of CO [29]. Compared to those of the G-CQDs and H-CQDs, the absorption peaks of the S-CQDs near 230 nm and 280 nm are weak, and the H-CQDs have the highest content of aromatic CC, CC, and CO. These results may be due to differences in the lignin precursor. Fig. 2 (c–e) shows the PL spectra of the G-CQDs, S-CQDs, and H-CQDs, and their QYs are 5.48 %, 3.28 %, and 1.86 %, respectively. The best excitation wavelength for the G-CQDs is 420 nm, and the corresponding emission wavelength is 509 nm. The optimal excitation wavelengths of the S-CQDs and H-CQDs are 430 and 370 nm, respectively, and their corresponding emission wavelengths are 504 and 462 nm, respectively. These results indicate that the G-CQDs, S-CQDs, and H-CQDs exhibit the typical excitation-dependent behavior of CQDs. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission wavelengths of the three CQDs exhibit a redshift. Some researchers [30] believe that this is mainly related to the multiple emission centers generated by the various functional groups of the CQDs. Meanwhile, some researchers [31] also hold that this might be related to the particle size homogeneity of the CQDs. CQDs of the same particle size has a specific excitation wavelength. However, some studies [32] deem that this is related to the “solvent relaxation” phenomenon of graphene-like CQDs in polar solvents (such as water) because CQDs usually do not exhibit excitation dependence in non-polar solvents. The G-CQDs exhibit the strongest fluorescence intensity among the three CQDs, and the H-CQDs exhibit the weakest. Fig. 2 (f–h) shows the up-conversion PL (up-PL) spectra of the G-CQDs, S-CQDs, and H-CQDs. When the three CQDs are irradiated with near-infrared light of 780–870 nm, they emit visible light of 450–550 nm, achieving anti-Stokes luminescence, which means that a long-wavelength excitation can cause a short wavelength emission. However, because of the low fluorescence intensity, the practical applications of the CQDs may be limited. It can be observed from Fig. 2 (c–h) that the S-CQDs demonstrate fluorescence properties, and their excitation wavelength dependence is identical to the traditional CQDs, which indicates that a small number of S-type models realized the polymerization of benzene rings on a three-dimensional level, and a small amount of S-CQDs-12 was generated. However, they could not be observed in the TEM field due to the small sample size. At present, it is not possible to determine the critical lignin units that dominate the formation of lignin-based CQDs only by topological and PL properties, and further chemical characterization of the three CQDs is required.

The FTIR spectra of the three lignin-based CQDs are shown in the Fig. 3 (a) and (b) depict the presence of OH, CH, CO, CC, COH, COC, and CH groups in the three types of CQDs, which is consistent with the UV–vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), compared to the G-CQDs and H-CQDs, the S-CQDs have a stronger OH peak. This result might be because the cleavage of the bismethoxy group of the S-type lignin model which increases the number of hydroxyl groups. However, monomer polymerization caused by the hydroxyl group of the S-type lignin model leads to the disappearance of the hydroxyl group. Nevertheless, the polymerization effect of the S-type lignin model is weak, so a large number of its origin hydroxyl groups formed by the cleavage of the bismethoxy group are retained. Fig. 4 shows the XPS spectra of the G-CQDs, S-CQDs, and H-CQDs. From the full-scan spectra in Fig. 4 (a), (d), and (g), two peaks can be observed at 285 eV and 532 eV [33], indicating that the three CQDs were mainly composed of C and O. H-CQDs have the lowest oxygen content, mainly because the H-type lignin model precursor, which formed H-CQDs, does not contain side-chain methoxy groups. As shown in Fig. 4 (b), (e), and (h), the high-resolution C 1s spectra of the three CQDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 283.0 eV, 284.5 eV, and 287.0 eV, corresponding to graphitic carbon CC/CC, CO, and CO, respectively [34]. Moreover, the H-CQDs have the highest graphitic carbon content (76.19 %) among the three CQDs, indicating that the H-CQDs have the highest degree of graphitization and the complete CQD structure. In contrast, the S-CQDs have the lowest graphitic carbon content (43.70 %) among the three CQD samples, and the CO content is higher (53.89 %), demonstrating that multilayer graphene sheets might not stack a complete structure of CQDs in the S-CQDs. These results are similar to those shown in Fig. 2 (a). Fig. 4 (c), (f), and (i) is the high-resolution O 1s spectra of three CQDs, which can be deconvolved into two peaks at 530.0 eV and 531.6 eV, corresponding to CO and CO, respectively [35]. Among these, the S-CQDs have the highest content of CO structure, which might be due to the fact that the S-type lignin model has two methoxy groups/aromatic unit and it is difficult to break the methoxy side chain under the catalysis of H+ in the HTC reaction. Therefore, most of the original CO bonds in the S-type lignin model are retained. The CO structure in the three CQD samples is mainly derived from the carboxyl group in the propane branch chain of the model (ferulic acid, erucic acid, and coumaric acid), the ester groups produced by the carboxyl group, and the para-hydroxyl groups of other monomers under the catalysis of H+ in the HTC process.

In summary, owing to the good degree of graphitization and complete CQD structure in the CQDs formed by the G-type and H-type lignin models, G-type and H-type lignins were identified as the primary units that formed lignin-based CQDs. However, because G-CQDs-12 had better fluorescence performance than H-CQDs-12, it was concluded that the G-type lignin unit is the key lignin unit that dominated the formation of lignin-based CQDs. In addition, because the G-type lignin unit occupies a relatively large proportion of natural lignin, it is a typical representative of lignin. In a subsequent experiment, the G-type lignin model was used to explore the interaction effect of the lignin model and xylan on the formation of lignocellulose-based CQDs in an HTC system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of lignin model on xylan-based CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The primary method to study the formation of CQDs is to explore the physicochemical and optical properties of the obtained CQDs from the time dimension, which can improve the preparation system of CQDs and increase their theoretical applicability. Fig. 5 shows photographs of the hydrolysate of xylan and the mixture of xylan with the G-type lignin model after removing insoluble solids at different reaction times (2–12 h) during the HTC treatment. Fig. 5 (a) shows that the solution is light yellow when the HTC time is 2 h. When the time is increased from 2 h to 4 h and then to 10 h, the color of the solution gradually darkens. However, when the reaction time is extended to 12 h, the color of the solution becomes clear again and turns yellow. The combined experimental observations imply this might be because the reaction in the first 2 h is mainly xylan depolymerization into xylose. Then further decomposition of xylose into furfural, levulinic acid, formic acid, and other small molecules makes the solution color relatively light. When the reaction time is extended from 2 h to 10 h, under high temperature and high pressure, some small molecules are aggregated and carbonized and might form X-CQDs. The small‑carbonized molecules have a small particle size within a specific reaction time and can be stably dispersed in water, resulting in a deep solution color. When the reaction time is extended to 12 h, the particle size of the carbonized small molecules reaches a critical value, and the particles can no longer be stably dispersed in the solvent and form a precipitate, which is removed in the filtration purification stage; thus, the solution becomes clear again. However, it should be noted that the solution color is still darker than that of the 2 h sample. In addition to X-CQDs, this data shows that there are still some low-molecular-weight carbonized small molecules in the solution. Compared with Fig. 5 (a), it can be observed that when the mixture of xylan and G-type lignin model is treated in the reaction, as shown in Fig. 5 (b), regardless of the length of the reaction time, the color of the hydrolysate is the same, which is pale yellow. This result indicates that the addition of the G-type lignin model might re-design the reaction path of xylan degradation, making it possible to proceed toward the direction of CQD formation. This result might also be the reason why the natural lignocellulose-based CQDs did not undergo excessive carbonization under long-term HTC conditions, and the color of the hydrolysate was not too dark. The effect of the interaction between lignin and xylan on the formation of lignocellulose-based CQDs in the HTC process will be further elaborated.

To explore the formation of X-CQDs-X and GX-CQDs-X under different HTC time nodes, the micro morphologies of the two groups of samples were examined using TEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . Nano Measurer software was used to test the size of 150 CQD particles in each sample to obtain the particle size distribution and average particle size. The results are presented in Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), when the HTC time is only 2 h, X-CQDs-2 present an apparent CQD particle structure with an average size of 3.07 nm. This data indicates that a certain degree of polymerization also occurs during xylan degradation. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image shows that the appearance of X-CQDs-2 is irregular. However, as the HTC time increases from 2 h to 10 h (Fig. 6 (b–e)), the average diameter of X-CQDs-4, X-CQDs-6, X-CQDs-8, and X-CQDs-10 increases from 18.09 nm to 32.72 nm. The size of these CQDs is larger than that of general CQDs (<10 nm), which may be due to the aggregation of degradation products and carbonized fragments formed by further carbonization of xylan around the CQDs. As time increases, the size of the aggregated particles gradually increases, leading to an increase in the size of X-CQDs-X. When the reaction time is further increased to 12 h (Fig. 6 (f)), the X-CQDs-12 are more spherical than X-CQDs-2, and their average particle size is reduced to 3.17 nm. This might be because, as the reaction time increases, the diameter of the aggregated particles is too large to be stably dispersed in water and removed. Consequently, the CQDs are separated from the aggregate, and the normal diameter of the CQDs is restored. However, X-CQDs-12 do not exhibit a graphite-like lattice structure, indicating that the degradation products of xylan are difficult to aromatize and form an ordered structure in the HTC reaction [36,37]. These results are similar to the observations for xylan hydrolysate (Fig. 5). When the lignin model and xylan are used together for the HTC reaction, as shown in Fig. 6 (g–l), evenly distributed uniform spherical nanoparticles appear in all samples. This effect is due to the synergistic effect of hydrophilic groups (such as hydroxyl, aldehyde, and carboxyl) and hydrophobic groups (such as phenyl and alkoxy) on the surface of GX-CQDs-X [38]. With the increase in HTC time from 2 h to 12 h, the average particle size of the sample increases from 2.58 nm to 3.42 nm, as shown in Fig. 6 (g–l). When the HTC time is 2 h (Fig. 6 (g)), HR-TEM results show that the surface of GX-CQDs-2 is wholly covered by lattice fringes of 0.21 nm width, which corresponds to the diffraction surface of graphene (100) [39], indicating that GX-CQDs-2 have a highly graphitized structure. This is mainly caused by self-polymerization/condensation of the lignin model. However, as the reaction time increases from 2 to 10 h, as shown in Fig. 6 (h–k), a small number of amorphous structures appear on the highly graphitized surface of the CQDs. Because it is challenging to aromatize xylan degradation products into ordered structures, the amorphous structure should originate from the xylan degradation products. As a result, a “sunflower” structure with a highly graphitized core surrounded by an amorphous shell is formed. However, when the reaction time is extended to 12 h, lattice fringes cannot be observed on the surface of GX-CQDs-12, but the internal graphitized carbon core is still clearly visible. This result indicates that the amorphous structure completely covers the highly graphitized carbon core. Fig. S2 shows the SAED patterns of G-CQDs-12, X-CQDs-12 and GX-CQDs-12. As shown in Fig. S2 (a) and (c), both G-CQDs-12 and GX-CQDs-12 have obvious diffraction spots and rings, indicating that these two kinds of CQDs have significant crystalline properties. However, the X-CQDS-12 in Fig. S2 (b) do not exhibit crystal properties and demonstrate amorphous carbon structure. Compared with GX-CQDs-12, the G-CQDs-12 have more diffraction spots and more obvious diffraction rings, indicating that the G-CQDs-12 have a higher degree of graphitization. This may be because that the amorphous structure wraps around the graphitized core of GX-CQDS-12, which weakens their overall crystallization degree. The above results show that the lignin model effectively inhibits the carbonization of xylan, which allows more xylan degradation products to participate in the formation of CQDs within 2–12 h rather than further carbonization. This result also explains why all the GX-CQDs-X solutions remained clear and transparent (Fig. 5). Meanwhile, the highly graphitized core formed by the lignin model provides growth sites for xylan degradation products, making the CQD particles more uniform and improving their homogeneity and mass yield.

To further elucidate the effect of lignin in the HTC process on the formation of xylose-based CQDs, the surface element and functional group content changes of X-CQDs-X and GX-CQDs-X at different time nodes were explored using XPS and FTIR spectra. Fig. S3 shows the XPS spectra of X-CQDs-X. The full-scan spectra (Fig. S3 (a, d, g, j, m, p)) shows that all X-CQDs-X are composed of carbon and oxygen. Because the degradation products of glycan compounds have no aromatic structure and it is difficult for carbohydrate degradation products to undergo aromatization reactions, the carbon skeleton is mainly CC/CC. When the HTC time is 2 h, the carbon content of X-CQDs-2 is relatively low (63.05 %), the carbon skeleton content remains at 53.26 % (<60 %), and the carbon‑oxygen bond content is relatively high. This data indicates that a relatively complete structure of the CQDs was formed at 2 h [40]. From the C 1s spectra (Fig. S3 (b, e, h, k, n, q)), it can be observed that with increasing HTC time from 2 h to 10 h, the carbon content of the CQDs gradually increases (from 63.05 % to 76.83 %), and the carbon skeleton content exceeds over 65 %. Meanwhile, the dehydration reaction during the carbonization of xylan significantly reduces the carbon‑oxygen bond content and increases the carbon content; thus, the products at this time are mainly aggregated amorphous carbonized small molecules that grow around the formed CQDs. Beyond 10 h, these carbonized small molecules polymerize further to form an insoluble precipitate which is finally filtered. At this time, the soluble CQDs are stably dispersed in water and regain their dominant position, which is retained in the dialysis process; thus, the carbon skeleton content of the products recovers to 57.31 % (Fig. S3 (q)). The O 1s spectra show that the carbon and oxygen bond in all samples is CO and CO, and their content changes depict no obvious regularity (Fig. S3 (c, f, i, l, o, r)). These results are consistent with the macro analysis of xylan degradation during the HTC process (Fig. 5). The depolymerization of xylan mainly occurs in the first 2 h. Moreover, 2–10 h is the duration for further carbonization of small molecules derived from the degradation of xylan, leading to the formation of carbon particles that can be stably dispersed in water; thus, the color of the liquid products becomes darker. In 10–12 h duration, the carbon particles further polymerize to form precipitates and are removed; thus, the solution returns to yellow and clear again.

Fig. S4 shows the XPS spectra of GX-CQDs-X. From the full-scan spectrum (Fig. S4 (a, d, g, j, m, p)), it can be observed that all GX-CQDs-X are also composed of carbon and oxygen elements. When the HTC time increases from 2 h to 12 h, the overall elemental content of GX-CQDs-X does not change significantly, and the content of carbon and oxygen elements is stable at 3:7. The C 1s spectra (Fig. S4 (b, e, h, k, n, q)) show that GX-CQDs-2 has the highest carbon skeleton content, which might be due to the preferential aromatic repolymerization of the aromatic lignin model in HTC process, forming a carbon skeleton core with a high degree of graphitization. The carbon skeleton should be formed by CC and CC together. At this time, xylan mainly undergoes degradation reactions. As the reaction time increases, combined with Fig. 6, it can be inferred that small molecule products of xylose degradation gradually grow around the carbon core, increasing the defect structure such as CO, COH, and COC and a decrease in the relative content of the carbon skeleton. Meanwhile, as the time prolongs, the CO content of GX-CQDS-X gradually increases. It might be due to the abundant methoxy groups in the G-type lignin model and the higher content of CO bonds in the degradation products of xylan (such as xylose, furfural, formic acid, and levulinic acid) compared to CO.

The results of the XPS analysis were further verified using FTIR to analyze the structures of G-CQDs-12, X-CQDS-12, and GX-CQDS-12. As shown in Fig. 7 (a) and (b), both X-CQDs-12 and GX-CQDs-12 contain OH, CH, CO, and CO structures. Compared with those of G-CQDs-12, the peak positions of X-CQDs-12 and GX-CQDs-12 are basically the same and include the stretching vibration of OH (3374 cm−1) [41], stretching vibration, and out-of-plane bending vibration of CH (2980 and 1172 cm−1) [42], stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of COH and COC (1391, 1270, and 1076 cm−1) [20,43,44], and stretching vibration of CO (1760 cm−1) [40]. These results are consistent with those of the XPS analysis. However, some are shifted, indicating that their surface states have changed.

The chemical analysis results were further verified using Raman spectroscopy. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) reflects the degree of disorder of the CQDs relative to the perfect graphite structure material [45]. The smaller the value, the higher the degree of graphitization of the sample. It can be observed in Fig. S5 (a, f) that both X-CQDs-2 and X-CQDs-12 have weak G bands, indicating a small amount of graphite. However, the disappearance of the G band in the X-CQDs-4, X-CQDs-6, X-CQDs-8, and X-CQDs-10 samples indicates that disordered carbon is the main component in these CQD samples (Fig. S5 (b–e)). This result also verifies that the carbon skeleton of X-CQDs-X in the XPS analysis is mainly CC disordered carbon. All the GX-CQDs-X have D and G bands, as shown in Fig. S5 (g–l). In 2–12 h duration, the graphitization degree of the samples gradually decreases due to the growth of xylan degradation products on the surface of the carbon core, resulting in a gradual reduction in the relative content of the defect structure and the degree of graphitization. This data is consistent with the XPS results. GX-CQDs-2 have the smallest ID/IG value, indicating the highest degree of graphitization. This result also verifies that polymerization of the lignin model occurs in the first 2 h, and at this point, a highly graphitized carbon core is formed. As mentioned above, combined with the macroscopic results (Fig. 5), it can be inferred that when a mixture of lignin and xylan is used as a precursor for a common HTC reaction, the depolymerization of the xylan macromolecular chain and the nucleation process of the lignin-based CQDs occur significantly in the first 2 h. Then, the carbon core acts as a growth site, and these small molecules derived from xylan degradation gradually grow around it, reducing the graphitization degree of the overall structure and providing more surface defects, which might lead to an increase in fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile, the excessive carbonization of xylan is inhibited (all hydrolysate samples in Fig. 5 (b) remained clear). Therefore, the addition of the lignin model facilitates the directional transformation of hemicellulose into CQDs. A schematic of the main principle of the effect of lignin on xylan-based CQDs is shown in Scheme 2 .

After the physical and chemical properties of X-CQDs-X and GX-CQDs-X were explored at different time nodes, both X-CQDs-12 and GX-CQDs-12 were selected to test their optical properties and verify the interaction effect of the lignin model and xylan on the fluorescence characteristics of the CQDs. Fig. 8 (a) shows the UV–Vis spectra of G-CQDs-12, X-CQDs-12, and GX-CQDs-12. Compared with GX-CQDs-12 and G-CQDs-12, X-CQDs-12 do not show apparent absorption near 220 nm and 280 nm, indicating that X-CQDs-12 has low aromaticity and a low content CC and CO, and they might be carbon nanodots (CNDs) [27]. Moreover, compared with X-CQDs-12, GX-CQDs-12 contain more aromatic CC, CC, and CO structures, indicating that the existence of the G-type lignin unit is more beneficial to the formation of CQDs in the same HTC system than xylan alone. Fig. 8 (c–e) shows the PL spectra of X-CQDs-12, GX-CQDs-12, and G-CQDs-12, with QYs of 2.36 %, 13.52 %, and 5.48 %, respectively. The X-CQDs-12 and GX-CQDs-12 also show the same excitation dependence as that of the lignin unit model-based G-CQDs-12. Compared with that of G-CQDs-12, the optimal excitation wavelengths of X-CQDs-12 and GX-CQDs-12 are blue- and red-shifted, respectively. Meanwhile, compared to the fluorescence intensity of G-CQDs-12 (508) and X-CQDs-12 (317), GX-CQDs-12 has a higher fluorescence intensity (1170), demonstrating that the G-type model substance contributes to the formation of CQDs, and GX-CQDs-12 have greater practical applicability. Fig. 8 (f–h) shows the up-PL spectra of X-CQDs-12, GX-CQDs-12, and G-CQDs-12, respectively. The up-conversion PL intensity of X-CQDs-12 is even lower than that of G-CQDs-12, and the up-PL intensity of GX-CQDs-12 is significantly improved. Moreover, when the GX-CQDs-12 are excited with 780 nm near-infrared light, they generate visible blue-green light with a wavelength of 480 nm and an up-PL intensity of 547. Even when the GX-CQDs-12 are excited by near-infrared light with a wavelength of 870 nm, it can still produce visible green light with a wavelength of 514 nm and an intensity of 193, which is still much higher than that of both X-CQDs-12 (77) and G-CQDs-12 (92). These results show that, compared to X-CQDs-12 and G-CQDs-12, GX-CQDs-12 has better application potential in the conversion of low-frequency invisible light to visible light, such as infrared imaging. In addition, it can be concluded from the above results that the existing G-type lignin model in xylan can significantly improve the fluorescence performance of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of GX-CQDs-12 in in-vivo imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity of GX-CQDs-12 sample on Hela cells was first tested by CKK-8 method at the concentrations of 0.1, 0.5 and 1.0 mg/mL, respectively, as shown in Fig. S6. With the concentration increase of GX-CQDs-12 in medium, the cell viability gradually decreased. Even when the concentration of GX-CQDs-12 reached 1 mg/mL, the cell viability still exceeded over 70 %. These results indicate that the GX-CQDs-12 have good biocompatibility and cytotoxicity.

After cytotoxicity tests, GX-CQDs-12 saline solution was intravenously injected into tumor-implanted nude mice, and the fluorescence signals in vivo were observed at different times. The results are shown in Fig. 9 (a). No obvious signal can be observed after 1 h of injection (Fig. 9 (a), 1 h). However, after 2 h of injection, fluorescent signals appear in the neck, thoracic cavity, and tumors of the mice (Fig. 9 (a), 2 h). As the time after injection is extended to 4–8 h (Fig. 9 (a), 4–8 h), the distribution of the fluorescence signal gradually spreads but is still mainly concentrated in the thoracic cavity and tumor site. After 12–24 h of injection, the intensity of the fluorescence signal gradually weakens, but the tumor site still maintains a strong signal (Fig. 9 (a), 12–24 h). The above results indicate that GX-CQDs-12 can be rapidly metabolized in the normal tissues of mice. Nude mice were euthanized after 24 h of injection, and the main organs were taken to obtain fluorescence images. As shown in Fig. 9 (b), the fluorescence signal is mainly concentrated at the tumor site and liver, whereas no signal is evident in other organs. These results indicate that GX-CQDs-12 are mainly metabolized by the liver, accumulated stably at the tumor site, and have good fluorescence stability at the tumor site. GX-CQDS-12 has excellent potential for in-vivo biological imaging and can be used as a tumor marker reagent. In addition, another nude mouse remained alive even after seven days of injection of GX-CQDs-12 saline, indicating that GX-CQDs-12 has good biocompatibility for in-vivo imaging.

The G-type model dominates the physicochemical properties and fluorescence performance of lignin-based CQDs. In the hydrothermal system of lignin and xylan, lignin preferentially undergoes a nucleation reaction at the beginning of 2 h, which provides growth sites for LC-CQDs formation. At this time, the graphitization of GX-CQDs is the highest, and xylan only simply degraded. As time increase, xylan degrades into small molecules that gradually grow around the core, thus forming the “sunflower” structure of CQDs. Moreover, the lignin inhibits the carbonization of xylan degradation products. GX-CQDs-12 have excellent biocompatibility and are suitable for labeling tumors.

Peitao Zhao: Data curation, Visualization. Chuanling Si: Validation, Software. Ting Xu: Validation. Chen Huang: Validation.
